results.question: Quantum Tech Pvt Ltd (Karnataka GSTIN) receives a purchase order from a UK buyer. The UK buyer asks Quantum Tech to deliver the goods directly to a Mumbai-based third party (the UK buyer's Indian agent). Quantum Tech raises the invoice on the UK buyer in USD and receives an advance of USD 50,000. Determine: (a) place of supply for the goods, (b) whether this is export of goods, (c) tax treatment of the advance.
results.answer_preview:
The place of supply for the goods supplied by Quantum Tech Pvt Ltd to the UK buyer through a Mumbai-based third party is the location of the goods at the time the movement of goods terminates for delivery to the recipient [S6].  

The supply of goods by Quantum Tech Pvt Ltd to the UK buyer through a Mumbai-based third party is considered an export of goods if the goods are moved to a port or customs station for export [S5].  

In conclusion, the place of supply is determined by the location of the goods at the time of delivery to the recipient, and the transaction may qualify as an export if the goods are subsequently moved to a port or customs station for export.

results.question: A gift hamper contains chocolates, a fruit juice bottle, and a toy, sold at one price for Diwali. Composite or mixed supply?
results.answer_preview: A gift hamper containing chocolates, a fruit juice bottle, and a toy, sold at one price for Diwali, is considered a mixed supply under the CGST Act, 2017. This is because each item within the hamper can be supplied separately and is not dependent on any other item. As stated, "aerated drinks and fruit juices when supplied for a single price is a mixed supply. Each of these items can be supplied separately and is not dependent on any other. It shall not be a mixed supply if these items are supplied separately;" [S1]. Therefore, the supply of such a gift hamper constitutes a mixed supply under the CGST Act, 2017.

results.question: The supplier has not uploaded the invoice in GSTR-1 but the recipient has the tax invoice in hand. Can the recipient claim ITC?

The recipient must also have received the goods or services and made payment for the amount towards the value of such goods or services [S2]. Therefore, even if the recipient has the tax invoice in hand, the absence of the supplier uploading the invoice in GSTR-1 does not automatically disqualify the recipient from claiming ITC, provided the other conditions—receipt of goods/services and payment—are fulfilled. However, the supplier's failure to upload the invoice in GSTR-1 may affect the verification process and the availability of the invoice for ITC purposes. results.question: Is ITC available on GST paid for office renovation of a rented building (capitalized in books)?
results.answer_preview:
Input tax credit (ITC) of the GST paid on inputs, including inward supplies of stores and spares, packing materials etc., shall be available as ITC as long as these inputs are used for the purpose of the business and/or for effecting taxable supplies, including zero-rated supplies, and the ITC for such inputs is not restricted under section 17(5) of the CGST Act [S1].  

Capital goods have been clearly defined in section 2(19) of the CGST Act as goods whose value has been capitalized in the books of account and which are used or intended to be used in the course or furtherance of business. Stores and spares, the expenditure on which has been charged as a revenue expense in the books of account, cannot be held to be capital goods [S1].  

In conclusion, ITC on GST paid for office renovation...

results.question: A supplier of goods receives an advance of Rs 5 lakhs on 10 April 2025 against a future supply. Is GST payable on the advance?
results.answer_preview:
Is GST payable on advances received by a supplier of goods against future supply under the GST law? The verbatim span states: "Aggregate value of all outward supplies net of debit notes / credit notes, net of advances and net of goods returned for the entire financial year shall be declared here." [S1] This indicates that advances are netted off from the aggregate value of outward supplies, implying that GST is not payable on advances received against future supply until the actual supply is made.

Conclusion: GST is not payable on advances received by a supplier of goods against future supply until the actual supply is made.

results.question: What is the time of supply when an advance is received for a service?
results.answer_preview:
The time of supply when an advance is received for a service is determined based on whether the supply has been completed. If the supply has been completed, the time of supply is when the payment was received in advance. If the supply has not been completed, the advance is adjusted accordingly [S5]. 

In conclusion, the time of supply for a service involving an advance payment depends on the completion status of the service. If the service is completed, the time of supply corresponds to when the advance was received; if not, the advance is adjusted in accordance with the relevant provisions [S5].

results.question: A registered company pays a sole-proprietor advocate for legal services. Who is liable to pay GST?
results.answer_preview:
When a registered company engages a sole-proprietor advocate for legal services, GST is required to be paid by the recipient of the service under the reverse charge mechanism, i.e. the business entity. [S1]  

The registered company, as the recipient of the legal services, is liable to pay GST under the reverse charge mechanism.  

In conclusion, the registered company is liable to pay GST when it engages a sole-proprietor advocate for legal services, as the recipient of the service is responsible under the reverse charge mechanism.

results.question: A manufacturer has accumulated ITC because its output tax rate (5%) is lower than input tax rates (18%). Can it claim a refund, and under what provision?
results.answer_preview:
The manufacturer can claim a refund of accumulated input tax credit under section 54(3) of the CGST Act read with rule 89(5) of the CGST Rules [S2]. This provision is applicable in situations where the output tax rate is lower than the input tax rate, allowing for the refund of the accumulated ITC. 

In conclusion, the manufacturer is entitled to claim a refund of accumulated input tax credit under section 54(3) of the CGST Act read with rule 89(5) of the CGST Rules.

results.question: What is the threshold value above which an e-way bill is mandatory for inter-state movement of goods?
results.answer_preview:
The threshold value above which an e-way bill is mandatory for inter-state movement of goods is fifty thousand rupees [S1]. 

This conclusion is directly derived from the verified fact provided, which specifies that an e-way bill is required for the movement of goods with a consignment value exceeding fifty thousand rupees, whether in relation to a supply, for reasons other than supply, or due to inward supply from an unregistered person. 

In conclusion, the threshold value for mandatory e-way bill generation for inter-state movement of goods is fifty thousand rupees.

results.question: What is duty drawback under Section 74 of the Customs Act?
results.answer_preview:
Section 74 of the Customs Act provides for the allowance of duty drawback on the re-export of duty-paid goods. Specifically, when goods that have been imported into India and upon which any duty has been paid on importation are entered for export, and the proper officer makes an order permitting clearance and loading of the goods for exportation under section 51, or are to be exported as baggage and the owner of such baggage, for the purpose of [S1]. 

In conclusion, Section 74 of the Customs Act outlines the conditions under which duty drawback is allowable, primarily when duty-paid goods are re-exported, either for exportation under section 51 or as baggage.

results.question: What is the role of the Special Valuation Branch (SVB) in customs?
results.answer_preview:
The Special Valuation Branch (SVB) was created as an institution specializing in the investigation of transactions involving special relationships between buyer and seller or those involving other special circumstances surrounding the sale of imported goods, both of which have a bearing on the assessable value [S5]. 

In conclusion, the role of the SVB is to investigate transactions involving special relationships between buyers and sellers or other special circumstances surrounding the sale of imported goods, which have an impact on the assessable value.

results.question: Who can seek an advance ruling under the Customs Act and on what matters?
results.answer_preview: The eligible individuals to seek an advance ruling under the Customs Act are those who are registered or wish to obtain registration under the Act [S3]. Advance rulings can be sought on matters related to the classification of goods and/or services, applicability of notifications, and other issues related to the nature of activities such as manufacturing, wholesale, retail, service provision, etc. [S5]. In conclusion, only individuals registered or seeking registration under the Customs Act may apply for an advance ruling, and such rulings pertain to matters concerning the classification of goods and services, the applicability of notifications, and other issues related to the nature of business activities.

results.question: On import of goods into India, what are the components of customs duty levied?
results.answer_preview:
The components of customs duty levied on the import of goods into India are specified under the Customs Tariff Act, 1975, or any other law in force, and are levied at rates determined by these laws. Except as otherwise provided in this Act, or any other law for the time being in force, duties of customs shall be levied at such rates as may be specified under [the Customs Tariff Act, 1975 (51 of 1975)], or any other law for the time being in force, on goods imported into, or exported from, India [S2]. 

In conclusion, the components of customs duty on imports into India are determined by the Customs Tariff Act, 1975, or any other applicable law, and are subject to rates specified under these laws.

results.question: What is the definition of 'manufacture' under the Central Excise Act?

results.question: Is mere labeling or re-labeling of goods covered under 'deemed manufacture'?
results.answer_preview:
The activity of labeling or re-labeling of containers, when it renders the product marketable to the consumer, is deemed to be "manufacture" under Chapter Note 10 of Chapter 29 [S2]. 

In conclusion, mere labeling or re-labeling of goods may be covered under the concept of "deemed manufacture" if it results in the product becoming marketable to the consumer.

results.question: An SSI unit used another's brand name. Could it still claim the SSI exemption under 8/2003-CE?
results.answer_preview:
Using another's brand name may affect the eligibility of an SSI unit to claim the SSI exemption under 8/2003-CE. Specifically, if the rightful owner of the brand name has not voluntarily foregone their enforceable right in respect of such brand name, subject to the conditions as in the ANNEXURE I, the SSI unit may not be eligible to claim the exemption [S4]. 

In conclusion, the use of another's brand name could impact the SSI unit’s ability to claim the SSI exemption under 8/2003-CE, depending on whether the brand name owner has voluntarily waived their enforceable rights as outlined in ANNEXURE I.

results.question: Under the Place of Provision of Services Rules 2012, what was the default rule for determining place of provision?
results.answer_preview:
Under the Place of Provision of Services Rules 2012, the default rule for determining the place of provision is as follows: "In the case of any service which does not qualify as a service provided to an account holder, the place of provision will be determined under the default rule i.e. the Main Rule 3. Thus, it will be the location of the service receiver where it is known (ascertainable in the ordinary course of business), and the location of the service provider otherwise." [S1]

results.question: What is the anti-profiteering clause under GST and which body examines complaints now?
results.answer_preview:
The anti-profiteering clause under GST requires that a registered person must pass on the benefit of tax rate reductions or input tax credit to the recipient through a commensurate reduction in prices [S5]. 

The body that examines complaints related to the anti-profiteering clause is the Director General of Anti-profiteering, as referenced in the determination process outlined in the clause.

In conclusion, the anti-profiteering clause under GST mandates that registered persons pass on tax benefits to recipients through reduced prices, and the Director General of Anti-profiteering is the body responsible for examining complaints in this regard.

results.question: What is the time limit for filing an appeal to the First Appellate Authority under the CGST Act?
results.answer_preview:
The time limit for filing an appeal to the First Appellate Authority under the CGST Act is three months from the date the decision or order is communicated to the person aggrieved [S5].  

In conclusion, the time limit for filing an appeal to the First Appellate Authority under the CGST Act is three months from the date the decision or order is communicated to the person aggrieved.

results.question: What is the mandatory pre-deposit required to file an appeal with the Appellate Authority under GST?
results.answer_preview: If any person files an appeal in accordance with the requirement of sub-section (8) of section 112 of the CGST Act (i.e., on payment of prescribed pre-deposit), the recovery proceedings for the balance amount is deemed to be stayed till disposal of the appeal as per sub-section (9) of section 112 of the CGST Act [S4]. The mandatory pre-deposit is specified under sub-section (8) of section 112 of the CGST Act, though the exact amount is not provided in this span.
